title: Create a Custom Tool
lede: Custom Tools turn your AI agent into a doer, not just a talker — reusable HTTP actions it can call mid-conversation to look up an order, check an address, or push data into any system you own. Here's how to build, test, and attach one from A to Z.

steps.title: Open the Custom Tools Library
steps.content:
Every Custom Tool you build lives in one library, ready to be attached to any of your agents.

  1. In the left sidebar, scroll to the CALL CENTER section and click Custom Tools. (You can also reach it from the top bar: click the Contact Center tab, then the Custom Tools chip in the strip below.)
  2. The list shows every tool you've created, with these columns:
    • Name — the function name your agent calls.
    • Description — what the tool does.
    • Method — GET (green) or POST (blue).
    • URL — the endpoint it calls (click the copy icon to copy it).
    • Voice Settings — whether the agent speaks During and/or After the call to your endpoint.
    • Owner — which account (main or sub-account) owns the tool. Use the Filter by owner dropdown above the table to narrow the list.
    • Actions — the green eye opens the tool, the red trash deletes it.
  3. The API Info button in the top-right shows how to create and manage tools programmatically via the iMash API.

images.caption: The Custom Tools library — every HTTP tool your agents can use, in one place.
The Custom Tools library — every HTTP tool your agents can use, in one place.

steps.title: Start a New Tool
steps.content:
1. Click Add Custom Tool in the top-right corner of the list.
  2. The Create Custom Tool drawer slides in from the right with three sections: Basic Information, API Configuration (with the Parameter Schema Builder inside), and Voice Settings.
  3. You'll fill them in top to bottom in the next steps — nothing is saved until you click Create Tool at the bottom.

images.caption: The Create Custom Tool drawer: Basic Information, API Configuration, and the Parameter Schema Builder.
The Create Custom Tool drawer: Basic Information, API Configuration, and the Parameter Schema Builder.

steps.title: Name and Describe the Tool
steps.content:
These two fields are what the AI actually reads when deciding whether to use your tool — treat them as instructions, not labels.

  1. Under Basic Information, enter a Name (minimum 3 characters). Use a short, action-style name like lookupAddress, getOrderStatus, or createTicket.
  2. Enter a Description in plain language: what the tool does, and when the agent should call it. For example: "Looks up the status of a customer's order by order number. Use whenever the caller asks where their order is."
💡 Tip: A vague description is the #1 reason a tool never fires. Spell out the trigger condition ("use when the caller asks about…") and the AI will call it reliably.

images.alt: Basic Information section with Name and Description fields
images.caption: Name and Description — the AI reads both to decide when to call your tool.

images.alt: Basic Information section with Name and Description fields
images.caption: Name and Description — the AI reads both to decide when to call your tool.

steps.title: Point It at Your Endpoint
steps.content:
Now tell iMash where to send the request when the agent uses the tool.

  1. Under API Configuration, choose the Request Method:
    • GET — for fetching or looking up information; the parameter values are sent along with the request.
    • POST — for sending or creating data; the parameter values are sent as a JSON body.
  2. Enter Your URL — the full address of the endpoint on your server (it must be a valid URL, e.g. https://api.yourcompany.com/orders/status).
⚠️ Important: The endpoint must be reachable from the internet (not localhost). If your API needs authentication, use a URL that carries its key (for example a signed or token-in-path endpoint), since request headers aren't configurable here.

images.alt: API Configuration section with Request Method and URL
images.caption: Choose GET or POST and paste the endpoint URL your agent should call.

images.alt: API Configuration section with Request Method and URL
images.caption: Choose GET or POST and paste the endpoint URL your agent should call.

steps.title: Build the Parameters the AI Fills In
steps.content:
Parameters are the pieces of information the AI extracts from the conversation and sends to your endpoint — an order number, an email, a zip code. The Parameter Schema Builder lets you define them with a simple form, no JSON required.

  1. On the Form Fields tab, click + Add Field. A field card appears — add one card per piece of data you need.
  2. Fill in each field card:
    • Field Name — the technical key sent to your API (e.g. order_number). Use lowercase and underscores.
    • Display Name — a friendly label for the field.
    • Type — 📝 Text, 🔢 Number, #️⃣ Integer, ☑️ Yes/No, or 📋 List.
    • Description — tell the AI what to put here (e.g. "The customer's order number, usually 6 digits"). This is how it knows what to extract from the conversation.
    • Required field — check it and the AI must collect this value before calling the tool.
    • Format (Text fields only) — Plain Text, Email, URL, Date, Date & Time, or Password.
    • Min / Max (Number and Integer fields only) — allowed value range.
  3. Use the ↑ / ↓ arrows on a card to reorder fields, and the red trash icon to delete one (you'll be asked to confirm).
💡 Tip: Field descriptions do double duty — they guide the AI's extraction AND document your API. One good sentence per field is all it takes.

images.alt: Parameter Schema Builder with a field card open
images.caption: Each field card defines one parameter: name, type, description, and whether it's required.

images.alt: Parameter Schema Builder with a field card open
images.caption: Each field card defines one parameter: name, type, description, and whether it's required.

steps.title: Power Users: Switch to JSON Code
steps.content:
Prefer raw JSON Schema, or need something the visual form can't express (nested objects, enums with custom labels)?

  1. At the top-right of the Parameter Schema Builder, flip the switch from Visual Builder to JSON Code.
  2. A full code editor opens where you can write the JSON Schema directly — this is the exact schema the LLM uses to structure the tool call.
  3. If your JSON has a syntax error, a JSON Parse Error alert appears and the Create Tool button stays disabled until you fix it.
  4. Flip the switch back at any time — your fields convert between the two modes automatically (the switch is locked while the JSON is invalid).
💡 Tip: Start in the Visual Builder even if you're technical — build the fields, flip to JSON Code, and use the generated schema as your starting point.

steps.title: Test It Before Going Live
steps.content:
Don't wait for a live call to find out your endpoint is misbehaving — fire a test request right from the builder.

  1. In the Parameter Schema Builder, click the Test Tool tab (next to Form Fields).
  2. A test form appears with one input per parameter you defined. Fill in sample values — exactly what the AI would extract on a real call.
  3. Click Test Tool. iMash sends a real request to your URL (GET or POST, per your configuration).
  4. The Test Results panel shows the HTTP status and the response body your endpoint returned. Whatever it returns is what the agent will read back to the caller — so make sure the response contains the answer in clear text or JSON.
⚠️ Note: The Test Tool button stays disabled until Your URL is filled in. If the test fails, you'll see the HTTP error code (e.g. HTTP 404) or a network error message to debug against.

images.alt: Test Tool tab with a sample test form
images.caption: The Test Tool tab generates a form from your parameters so you can fire a real request at your endpoint.

images.alt: Test Tool tab with a sample test form
images.caption: The Test Tool tab generates a form from your parameters so you can fire a real request at your endpoint.

steps.title: Voice Settings and Save
steps.content:
API calls take a second or two — Voice Settings keep the conversation feeling alive while your endpoint works.

  1. Under Voice Settings, turn on Speaks for Execution to have the agent say something while the tool runs. A Say field appears — enter the line, e.g. "One moment while I look that up for you."
  2. Turn on Speak After Execution to have the agent say something right after the tool returns. Enter its Say line, e.g. "Thanks for waiting — here's what I found."
  3. Leave both off if you'd rather the agent respond naturally with the tool's results and nothing else.
  4. Click Create Tool at the bottom-right to save (or Cancel to discard). Your new tool appears in the list immediately.

images.alt: Voice Settings section with both speak toggles enabled
images.caption: Turn on either toggle and a Say field appears for the exact line the agent should speak.

images.alt: Voice Settings section with both speak toggles enabled
images.caption: Turn on either toggle and a Say field appears for the exact line the agent should speak.

steps.title: Attach the Tool to an Agent
steps.content:
A tool in the library does nothing until an agent carries it. Attaching takes seconds:

  1. In the left sidebar, go to INTELLIGENCE CENTER → Smart Agents and open the agent that should use the tool.
  2. Click the Tools / MCP button at the bottom of the editor to open the Tools & MCP panel.
  3. In the left column, scroll to the CUSTOM section — your new tool is listed there. Click the + next to it to attach it.
  4. Click Save, then use Test Agent and ask a question that should trigger the tool (e.g. "Where's my order 123456?") to confirm it fires and answers with live data.
💡 Tip: The same tool can be attached to as many agents as you like — build once, reuse everywhere.

images.alt: Tools and MCP panel in the agent editor
images.caption: Your custom tools appear under CUSTOM in the agent's Tools & MCP panel — click + to attach.

images.alt: Tools and MCP panel in the agent editor
images.caption: Your custom tools appear under CUSTOM in the agent's Tools & MCP panel — click + to attach.

steps.title: Edit or Delete a Tool Later
steps.content:
1. Back on the Custom Tools list, click any row (or its green eye icon) to open the Custom Tool Details drawer.
  2. It's the same three-section form — change the name, URL, parameters, or voice settings. The Save Tool button lights up as soon as you modify anything.
  3. Click Save Tool to apply your changes. Agents using the tool pick up the new configuration on their next call — no re-attaching needed.
  4. To remove a tool, click Delete Tool in the drawer (or the red trash icon in the list) and confirm. This action cannot be undone.
⚠️ Warning: Before deleting a tool, detach it from any agents that use it — otherwise those agents lose a capability their prompt may still reference.

images.alt: Custom Tool Details drawer for an existing tool
images.caption: Open any tool from the list to edit it — Save Tool activates the moment you change something.

images.alt: Custom Tool Details drawer for an existing tool
images.caption: Open any tool from the list to edit it — Save Tool activates the moment you change something.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content:
If you run into any issues or have questions:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io
  • 💬 Join our Discord community
  • 📚 Check out our API Reference
  • 🎥 Watch our video tutorials

metaTitle: Create a Custom Tool | iMash Documentation
metaDescription: Build a reusable HTTP Custom Tool your iMash AI agent can call mid-conversation: name it, configure the endpoint and method, define parameters with the visual schema builder, test it live, and attach it to an agent.
metaKeywords: custom tools, http tool, function calling, agent tools, api endpoint, json schema, parameter schema builder, test tool, imash
